items.q: Why are cities warmer than surrounding countryside?
items.correct: Dark surfaces absorb solar radiation, concrete and brick store heat, vehicles and buildings release heat, and lack of vegetation reduces cooling by evapotranspiration.
items.misconception: Because cities are closer to the sun due to tall buildings.
items.random_wrong: Because cities are always in warmer climates.
items.why: The 'closer to the sun' idea reveals misunderstanding of scale — tall buildings are negligible relative to the 150-million-km distance to the sun. The effect is local energy balance.

items.correct: Through weathering of parent rock, decomposition of organic matter, biological activity, climate influence and time — a slow process taking hundreds to thousands of years.
items.why: Soil is a complex mix of mineral and organic matter, water, air and living organisms. Students reduce it to ground rock, missing the crucial biological component.

items.q: How are fold mountains like the Himalayas formed?
items.correct: By the collision of two continental plates (India and Eurasia) where neither can subduct, so the crust crumples and is forced upwards over millions of years.
items.misconception: Fold mountains form when one plate slides under another into the mantle.
items.random_wrong: Fold mountains form from giant volcanoes erupting upwards.
items.why: Students confuse continent-continent collision with subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, so it crumples — distinct from oceanic-continental boundaries like the Andes.
Correct answer: Hess. “Wegener” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the Tohoku earthquake magnitude?
items.why: It's 9.0. “7.0” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How tall (m) was the Tohoku tsunami?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 m. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
